You are bootstrapping a new web UI project: web-sw-cor24-plsw -- a browser-based
PL/SW development environment (editor/compiler) running on the COR24 emulator
via WASM.

Follow these steps in order.

## Step 1: Learn agentrail

Run:
```
agentrail --help
```
Understand the commands available (init, status, next, begin, complete, plan,
history, add, setup, abort).

## Step 2: Study an existing web project with agentrail

Look at sibling projects under ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/ and ~/github/sw-embed/
that have `.agentrail/` directories. Good references:

- ~/github/sw-embed/web-sw-cor24-pcode/ (closest match -- COR24 web debugger)
- ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/web-tml24c/ (Lisp REPL on COR24)
- ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/web-tc24r/ (C compiler browser UI)

From one of these (preferably web-sw-cor24-pcode), study:
- CLAUDE.md -- the AgentRail Session Protocol and project structure
- .agentrail/saga.toml -- saga config
- .agentrail/plan.md -- the phased plan
- .agentrail/steps/*/step.toml -- step configs (number, slug, description, role, task_type)
- Cargo.toml -- dependencies (Yew 0.21, wasm-bindgen, web-sys, gloo, etc.)
- index.html -- Trunk directives, Catppuccin Mocha theme, layout
- scripts/ -- serve.sh, build-pages.sh patterns
- src/ -- Yew Component architecture (Component trait, Msg enum, html! macro)
- pages/ -- GitHub Pages output directory with .nojekyll
- build.rs -- build metadata and pre-processing patterns

Then create a CLAUDE.md for THIS project (~/github/sw-embed/web-sw-cor24-plsw/)
that follows the same pattern. It must include:
- The full AgentRail Session Protocol (6 steps: next, begin, work, commit, complete, stop)
- Project description (browser-based PL/SW development environment on COR24)
- Related projects (sw-cor24-plsw, sw-cor24-emulator, sw-cor24-assembler, etc.)
- Build commands (trunk via scripts, clippy, fmt)
- Available task types (rust-project-init, yew-component, wasm-build, etc.)
- Edition 2024, never suppress warnings

## Step 3: Understand the web UI tech stack

From studying the reference projects, note the established patterns:
- Rust edition 2024
- Yew 0.21 with CSR (client-side rendering only, no server backend)
- Trunk as the build tool (not webpack/Vite)
- wasm-bindgen + web-sys for browser APIs
- Path dependencies to sibling compiler/emulator repos (e.g., ../sw-cor24-plsw, ../sw-cor24-emulator)
- Catppuccin Mocha dark theme
- pages/ directory committed to git, deployed via GitHub Pages
- scripts/serve.sh for dev, scripts/build-pages.sh for release
- build.rs for compile-time pre-processing and metadata

The live demo will be served from the pages/ directory via GitHub Pages at a
URL like: https://softwarewrighter.github.io/web-sw-cor24-plsw/

## Step 4: Read the base project research

Read ~/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-plsw/docs/research.txt thoroughly. This is the
comprehensive design document for the PL/SW language. Key aspects:
- PL/S-inspired systems programming language for COR24
- Freestanding: no runtime requirement, direct OS service integration
- PL/I-like syntax with DCL/DECLARE and level-based declarations
- Transpiler approach: compiles to .s assembler (not .o object files)
- AST-aware macro preprocessor (m4-like, not CPP-style)
- Three levels of inline assembler escape hatch (asm! macro style)
- 7-layer compiler pipeline
- File types: .plsw source, .msw macro files
- COR24 ISA constraints: no floats, chars and ints only
- GEN blocks for register-level code generation
- Macro invocation syntax: ?GETMAIN(...), ?ESTAE(...), etc.
- Recovery/environment macros for exception-like handling
- Mixed listing output showing expanded source

Also read ~/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-plsw/Cargo.toml and any other docs/ files
to understand the current state of the base PL/SW project.

## Step 5: Ask questions

Before proceeding to documentation, ask me any questions you have about:
- Scope of the web UI (full IDE? minimal editor + compile? visual debugger?)
- Which PL/SW features to prioritize in the UI
- Desired UI layout (editor + output? multi-panel with AST view? listing viewer?)
- Whether the PL/SW compiler is ready to embed or needs stubbing
- Integration approach with cor24-emulator (compile -> assemble -> run pipeline)
- How to display the 7-layer pipeline stages in the UI
- Macro expansion visualization preferences
- Any preferences for the architecture

## Step 6: Create documentation

Based on everything you've learned, create these four documents in ./docs/:

### docs/architecture.md
High-level system architecture:
- Component diagram (Yew components, PL/SW compiler pipeline, assembler, COR24 emulator)
- Data flow (PL/SW source -> preprocess -> compile -> .s assembly -> assemble -> emulate -> output)
- WASM build pipeline (Trunk -> wasm-bindgen -> pages/)
- Path dependency structure to sibling repos
- How the 7-layer compiler pipeline maps to UI views
- Macro expansion visualization architecture

### docs/prd.md
Product requirements document:
- Target user and use case
- Core features (editor with PL/SW syntax highlighting, compilation, assembly listing, execution)
- Non-functional requirements (performance, browser compatibility, offline)
- What's in scope vs. out of scope for v1
- Success criteria

### docs/plan.md
Implementation plan (phased, matching agentrail saga step pattern):
- Phase 1: Scaffold Yew project, wire dependencies, basic Trunk build
- Phase 2: Editor panel with PL/SW syntax highlighting
- Phase 3: Compile and display assembly listing
- Phase 4: Wire assembler + emulator for execution
- Phase 5+: Additional features based on PRD (macro expansion view, debugger, etc.)
- Each phase should map to 1-2 agentrail saga steps

### docs/design.md
Detailed UI/UX design:
- Layout wireframe description (editor, listing, output panels)
- Catppuccin Mocha theme integration
- PL/SW syntax highlighting scheme (keywords, macros, GEN blocks, inline asm)
- Macro expansion toggle/view design
- Pipeline stage visualization
- Component hierarchy (Yew Component tree)
- Keyboard shortcuts for compile/run workflow

Workflow CLI for keeping AI agents on track

Usage: agentrail [OPTIONS] <COMMAND>

Commands:
  init      Initialize a new saga
  setup     Bootstrap a project: create saga, CLAUDE.md, and register domain
  add       Add a step to the saga (for maintenance mode or ad-hoc tasks)
  status    Show current saga state
  next      Output current step prompt and context for a fresh agent session
  begin     Mark the current step as in-progress
  complete  Complete current step, optionally define next step
  plan      View or update the saga plan
  history   Show all step summaries
  distill   Distill trajectories into a skill document
  run-loop  Auto-execute deterministic steps, pause at agent steps
  abort     Mark current step as blocked
  help      Print this message or the help of the given subcommand(s)

Options:
      --saga <SAGA>  Path to the project directory (default: current directory) [default: .]
  -h, --help         Print help
  -V, --version      Print version

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have comprehensive information. Let me compile the findings.

## Key Patterns from Reference Projects

Based on studying both `web-tml24c` and `web-tc24r`, here are the critical patterns to apply:

### 1. **CLAUDE.md Protocol - AgentRail Session Workflow (CRITICAL)**
Both reference projects follow an identical AgentRail session protocol:
```
agentrail next → agentrail begin → [WORK] → git commit → agentrail complete → [STOP]
```
Key points:
- Section: "CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)"
- Step prompt IS the instruction—don't ask confirmation
- Never continue after `agentrail complete`; new work belongs in next step
- After `agentrail complete`, record: `--summary`, `--reward`, `--actions` (or `--failure-mode -1` if failed)
- Both projects mark their work with `--done` when saga is finished

### 2. **Path Dependencies - Sibling Repository Structure**
Both use relative path dependencies to access sibling repos:

**web-tml24c pattern:**
```toml
cor24-emulator = { path = "../../sw-embed/cor24-rs", default-features = false }
cor24-isa = { path = "../../sw-embed/cor24-rs/isa", features = ["serde"] }
```

**web-tc24r pattern:**
```toml
# Local compiler crates (within same vibe-coding repo)
tc24r-span = { path = "../tc24r/components/core/crates/tc24r-span" }
tc24r-preprocess = { path = "../tc24r/components/frontend/crates/tc24r-preprocess" }
tc24r-lexer = { path = "../tc24r/components/frontend/crates/tc24r-lexer" }
tc24r-parser = { path = "../tc24r/components/frontend/crates/tc24r-parser" }
tc24r-codegen = { path = "../tc24r/components/backend/crates/tc24r-codegen" }

# Cross-repo dependency (to sw-embed)
cor24-emulator = { path = "../../sw-embed/cor24-rs", default-features = false }
```

Key insight: Path dependencies use relative `../` traversal to sibling repos, with comments grouping by logical origin.

### 3. **Cargo.toml Patterns**
**Edition:** `2024` (mandatory for both)

**web-tml24c specifics:**
- Includes `crate-type = ["cdylib", "rlib"]` for WASM + library dual mode
- Minimal deps: only Yew, wasm-bindgen, web-sys, gloo (no futures)
- Release profile optimizes for size: `opt-level = "z"`, `lto = true`

**web-tc24r specifics:**
- No `crate-type` override (default lib)
- Uses `wasm-bindgen-futures` and `gloo-timers` for async/timer support
- More compiler pipeline crates (tc24r-*)

### 4. **index.html Patterns**

**web-tml24c (theme-aware):**
- Catppuccin Mocha color palette hardcoded as CSS variables:
  ```css
  :root {
    --bg: #1e1e2e;
    --surface: #282840;
    --border: #3e3e5e;
    --text: #cdd6f4;
    --accent: #89b4fa;
    --green: #a6e3a1;
    --red: #f38ba8;
    --dim: #9399b2;
    --mono: "JetBrains Mono", "Fira Code", "Cascadia Code", monospace;
  }
  ```
- Full viewport layout: flex column, 100vh, header/footer with borders
- GitHub corner (octocat wave animation)
- `<link data-trunk rel="rust" data-bin="web-tml24c" />`

**web-tc24r (minimal):**
- Basic Catppuccin colors inline (no CSS vars)
- No footer, no GitHub corner
- Empty body tag (Yew mounts to it)
- `<link data-trunk rel="rust" data-wasm-opt="z" />`

### 5. **Build Scripts (web-tml24c)**
Uses shell scripts instead of calling trunk directly:

**serve.sh:**
```bash
trunk serve --port 9135
```

**build-all.sh:**
- Compiles all 5 REPL variants (bare/minimal/standard/full/scheme) from tml24c
- Uses tc24r CLI to compile `.c` → `.s` (assembly)
- Builds trunk to `dist/`, rsyncs to `pages/` (GitHub Pages)
- Preserves `.nojekyll` across rebuilds

Key note: web-tc24r uses `trunk serve/build` directly (no scripts)

### 6. **.agentrail Structure**
Both projects contain:
```
.agentrail/
├── domains.toml          # Points to agentrail-domain-coding
├── saga.toml             # name, status, current_step, created_at, plan_file
├── plan.md               # High-level saga description
├── step0-summary.md      # Initial summary
├── domains.toml          # Domain configuration
├── steps/
│   ├── 001-slug/
│   │   ├── step.toml     # number, slug, status, role, completed_at
│   │   ├── prompt.md     # Task description for Claude
│   │   └── summary.md    # What was accomplished
│   ├── 002-slug/
│   └── ...
├── sessions/             # JSONL session logs
└── trajectories/         # Trajectory recordings
```

### 7. **CLAUDE.md Sections**
Both reference projects include:

```markdown
# CLAUDE.md

[Protocol section - AgentRail workflow]

## Project
[One-line description]

## Related Projects
[List with relative paths]

## Available Task Types
[Comma-separated list of agentrail-domain-coding task types]
  Examples: `rust-project-init`, `rust-clippy-fix`, `yew-component`, `wasm-build`, `pre-commit`, `c-compile-fix`, `rust-test-write`

## Build
[Instructions: scripts vs. direct trunk calls, edition, clippy, optimization]
```

### 8. **Unique Differences**

| Aspect | web-tml24c | web-tc24r |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| **Build** | Uses `./scripts/` wrapper scripts | Direct `trunk serve/build` |
| **Library mode** | WASM + library (`cdylib`, `rlib`) | Default library only |
| **Dependencies** | Minimal (cor24-emulator, yew, gloo) | More (tc24r-* compiler crates) |
| **Async** | Single sync REPL evaluator | Uses `wasm-bindgen-futures` + gloo-timers |
| **HTML** | Full themed layout + GitHub corner | Minimal empty shell |
| **Release size** | Aggressive: `opt-level = "z"` + LTO | Default |

---

**Bottom Line:** Your current project should adopt the AgentRail protocol in CLAUDE.md, use relative path dependencies for sibling repos, use Edition 2024, and choose between the minimal (web-tc24r) or scripted+themed (web-tml24c) build approach based on your needs.

## Summary of PL/SW Compiler Project Files

### 1. **research.txt** - Comprehensive Language Design Document

This extensive document (1,437 lines) is a detailed conversation between the developer and Claude about designing PL/SW. Key sections include:

**Initial Vision**: Recreation of IBM PL/S-family languages (PL/S-III, PL/AS, PL/X) with:
- PL/I-like syntax, not C
- Freestanding systems language
- Compile-time macro preprocessor (like m4, not C preprocessor)
- Transpiler to `.s` assembly, not object code
- Inline assembler escape hatches (like Rust's `asm!` macro)

**Recommended Architecture**:
- Core PL/SW language (relatively small, understandable without macros)
- `.msw` macro files containing service abstractions
- Two-layer approach: core language + macro/meta layer
- AST-aware macros (not text substitution)
- Transparent expansion visibility (mixed listings showing what macros generated)

**Key Design Decisions**:
- Use DCL/DECLARE with odd-numbered levels (1, 3, 5, etc.) for future union support
- Integer-only types matching COR24 ISA (no floats)
- Macro invocation: `?GETMAIN(LENGTH) SUBPOOL(SP) ADDRESS(ptr);`
- Three expansion categories: surface macros, structural macros, codegen macros
- GEN blocks for direct assembler generation within macros
- Recovery environment macros like `?ESTAE` for structured cleanup
- Entry/exit/linkage macros for procedure boilerplate

**Success Criteria for v1**:
- Service invocation macros (e.g., `?GETMAIN`)
- Entry/exit linkage handling
- Recovery environment establishment
- Data-layout macros
- Meta-macros (macros that define macros)

---

### 2. **docs/prd.md** - Product Requirements Document

Defines the PL/SW compiler project scope:

**Purpose**: Implement PL/SW compiler for COR24 24-bit RISC ISA, written in C, running natively on COR24

**Key Goals**:
1. Working compiler reading `.plsw` → emitting `.s` assembly
2. PL/I-like syntax (DCL, PROC, DO/END, IF/THEN/ELSE, levels)
3. Integer-only types (BIT, BYTE, WORD, INT(n), CHAR, PTR)
4. Macro preprocessor with `.msw` files and `?MACRO(...)` syntax
5. Inline assembly via `ASM DO; ... END;`
6. Readable `.s` output with source-line comments
7. Self-contained (runs on COR24 emulator, no host dependencies)

**Non-Goals** (explicitly deferred):
- Floating-point
- Full PL/I compatibility
- Sophisticated optimization
- Separate compilation/linking
- Exception handling (v1 uses macro-based recovery)

**Functional Requirements**:
- FR1: Source language with PL/I-inspired declarations and control flow
- FR2: Macro system with MACRODEF, GEN blocks, phase 1 (separate preprocessor), phase 2 (integrated)
- FR3: COR24 assembly generation with calling convention and register allocation
- FR4: Toolchain integration: `plsw input.plsw [-o output.s] [-I macrodir]`

---

### 3. **docs/architecture.md** - System Architecture

Describes the compiler pipeline:

**Pipeline Stages**:
1. Macro preprocessing (parse .msw, resolve includes, expand `?MACRO`)
2. Lexical analysis (tokenize, handle inline ASM blocks)
3. Parsing (recursive descent, AST node pool)
4. Semantic analysis (symbol table, type checking, record layout)
5. IR lowering (desugar control flow, lower array/record access)
6. Register allocation (linear-scan for r0-r2, spill to stack)
7. Assembly emission (COR24 .s with labels, directives, source comments)

**Key Data Structures**:
- Token: type, value, name
- ASTNode: kind, type_info, storage_class, level, tree pointers, name, int_val
- Symbol: name, type, width, storage, offset, level, scope

**COR24 Calling Convention**:
- Args pushed right-to-left (3 bytes each)
- Return in r0
- Callee saves r1, r2, fp
- Prologue/epilogue with frame pointer

**Compiler Limitations (v1)**:
- Single translation unit
- 3 GP registers (aggressive spilling)
- No floats
- Max ~64 symbols per scope, ~256 AST nodes

# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)

Every agent session follows this 6-step loop. Do NOT skip or reorder steps.

1. **`agentrail next`** -- Read the current step prompt. The step prompt IS the instruction. Do not ask for confirmation; just do it.
2. **`agentrail begin`** -- Mark the step as in-progress. Do this immediately after reading.
3. **WORK** -- Implement what the step describes. Follow all project conventions below.
4. **`git commit`** -- Commit your work with a descriptive message. All pre-commit quality gates must pass.
5. **`agentrail complete`** -- Mark the step done. Include `--summary "..."`, `--reward N` (1-10), and `--actions N` (number of tool calls). If the step failed, use `--failure-mode "..."` and `--reward -1`.
6. **STOP** -- Do not continue. New work belongs in the next step (next session).

## Project: web-sw-cor24-plsw -- PL/SW Development Environment on COR24

Browser-based PL/SW development environment (editor/compiler) running the PL/SW compiler pipeline on the COR24 emulator via WASM. Built with Yew 0.21 CSR + Trunk.

PL/SW is a PL/I-inspired freestanding systems programming language for the COR24 24-bit RISC ISA. The web UI provides an editor with PL/SW syntax highlighting, compilation to COR24 assembly, assembly listing view, and execution on the COR24 emulator.

## Related Projects

All COR24 repos live under `~/github/sw-embed/` as siblings:

- `sw-cor24-plsw` -- PL/SW compiler (C, runs on COR24 natively)
- `sw-cor24-emulator` -- COR24 assembler and emulator (Rust)
- `sw-cor24-assembler` -- COR24 assembler library
- `web-sw-cor24-assembler` -- COR24 assembly IDE (browser)
- `web-sw-cor24-pcode` -- P-code VM debugger (browser, closest reference project)
- `sw-cor24-pcode` -- P-code VM, assembler, linker (Rust workspace)
- `sw-cor24-rust` -- Rust-to-COR24 pipeline

## Build

Edition 2024 for all Rust code. Never suppress warnings.

```bash
trunk build                    # Build WASM to dist/
./scripts/serve.sh             # Dev server
./scripts/build-pages.sh       # Release build to pages/ for GitHub Pages
cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings  # Lint
cargo fmt --all                # Format
```

## Available Task Types

rust-project-init, yew-component, wasm-build, rust-clippy-fix, pre-commit, rust-test-write, css-styling, documentation

## Architecture

- **Trunk** builds the WASM binary and serves it
- **cor24-emulator** provides `EmulatorCore` + `Assembler` (path dep to `../sw-cor24-emulator`)
- **Yew 0.21** CSR framework for the UI
- Component-based architecture using `Component` trait with `Msg` enum for state updates
- `build.rs` for compile-time pre-processing and metadata embedding
- Catppuccin Mocha dark theme
- `pages/` directory committed to git, deployed via GitHub Pages
- PL/SW compilation pipeline: source -> preprocess -> compile -> .s assembly -> assemble -> emulate -> output

## Key Conventions

- Monospace font stack: JetBrains Mono, Fira Code, Cascadia Code
- CSS custom properties for Catppuccin Mocha colors
- Flexbox layout for responsive multi-panel design
- `include_bytes!()` / `include!()` for embedding build-time outputs
- Release profile: `opt-level = "z"`, `lto = true` for minimal WASM size
- GitHub Pages URL: https://softwarewrighter.github.io/web-sw-cor24-plsw/

input.content:
# Architecture: web-sw-cor24-plsw

## Overview

Browser-based PL/SW development environment running the full PL/SW compiler
pipeline on the COR24 emulator via WASM. The PL/SW compiler itself is written
in C and runs on COR24 natively -- the web UI hosts it by running the compiled
compiler binary on the COR24 emulator in WASM, the same approach used by the
cor24-rs C pipeline (web-tc24r fork).

## Component Diagram

```
+-----------------------------------------------------------+
|  Browser (WASM)                                           |
|                                                           |
|  +-----------------------------------------------------+ |
|  | Yew 0.21 CSR App                                    | |
|  |                                                     | |
|  |  [Tab: Editor]           [Tab: Pipeline]            | |
|  |                                                     | |
|  |  +-- Wizard Sidebar --+  +-- Pipeline Viewer -----+ | |
|  |  | 1. Source (.plsw)  |  | Stage 1: Preprocess    | | |
|  |  | 2. Macros (.msw)   |  | Stage 2: Lex           | | |
|  |  | 3. Preprocess      |  | Stage 3: Parse         | | |
|  |  | 4. Compile         |  | Stage 4: Semantic      | | |
|  |  | 5. Assemble        |  | Stage 5: IR Lower      | | |
|  |  | 6. Run/Debug       |  | Stage 6: Reg Alloc     | | |
|  |  +--------------------+  | Stage 7: Emit          | | |
|  |                          +------------------------+ | |
|  |  +-- Notebook Cells (scrolling) -----------------+  | |
|  |  | .plsw source editor                           |  | |
|  |  | .msw macro file editors                       |  | |
|  |  | Preprocessed PL/SW output                     |  | |
|  |  | COR24 .s assembly output                      |  | |
|  |  | Assembly listing + Debugger (run/step)         |  | |
|  |  +------------------------------------------------+ | |
|  +-----------------------------------------------------+ |
|                                                           |
|  +-----------------------------------------------------+ |
|  | cor24-emulator (EmulatorCore)                       | |
|  |   Runs PL/SW compiler binary (compiled from C)      | |
|  |   Then runs the compiled PL/SW program              | |
|  +-----------------------------------------------------+ |
+-----------------------------------------------------------+
```

## Data Flow

The pipeline has two execution phases on the emulator:

### Phase A: Compilation (PL/SW compiler running on COR24)

```
.plsw source + .msw macros
    |
    v
[COR24 Emulator running plsw compiler binary]
    |
    +-- Stage 1: Macro preprocessing (expand ?MACRO invocations)
    +-- Stage 2: Lexical analysis (tokenize PL/SW)
    +-- Stage 3: Parsing (recursive descent -> AST)
    +-- Stage 4: Semantic analysis (types, symbols)
    +-- Stage 5: IR lowering (desugar control flow)
    +-- Stage 6: Register allocation (linear-scan, r0-r2)
    +-- Stage 7: Assembly emission
    |
    v
COR24 .s assembly output (captured from emulator UART)
```

### Phase B: Assembly + Execution

```
COR24 .s assembly
    |
    v
[cor24-emulator Assembler (Rust, direct WASM call)]
    |
    v
Machine code binary + listing + labels
    |
    v
[COR24 Emulator running compiled program]
    |
    v
Output (UART TX), register state, memory view
```

## WASM Build Pipeline

```
Trunk
  |
  +-- Compiles Rust to WASM via wasm-bindgen
  +-- Bundles index.html + CSS
  +-- Outputs to dist/
  |
  +-- build-pages.sh: trunk build --release -> rsync to pages/
  |
  v
pages/ -> GitHub Pages (https://softwarewrighter.github.io/web-sw-cor24-plsw/)
```

## Path Dependency Structure

```
~/github/sw-embed/
  |
  +-- web-sw-cor24-plsw/     (this project)
  |     Cargo.toml depends on:
  |       cor24-emulator = { path = "../sw-cor24-emulator" }
  |       cor24-isa = { path = "../sw-cor24-emulator/isa" }
  |
  +-- sw-cor24-emulator/      (assembler + emulator)
  +-- sw-cor24-plsw/          (PL/SW compiler source in C)
  +-- sw-cor24-assembler/     (assembler library)
```

## Pre-Built Compiler Binary

The PL/SW compiler is written in C and compiled to COR24 assembly via tc24r,
then assembled to a binary. This binary is embedded in the WASM app at build
time via `build.rs`:

```
sw-cor24-plsw/src/*.c
    |  (tc24r: C -> COR24 .s)
    v
plsw-compiler.s
    |  (cor24 assembler at build time)
    v
plsw-compiler.bin  (embedded via include_bytes!)
```

At runtime, the web UI loads this binary into the COR24 emulator, feeds PL/SW
source via UART RX, and captures compiled .s assembly from UART TX.

## Macro Expansion Visualization

The preprocessor stage is surfaced in the UI with collapsible expansion:

- Original .plsw source with `?MACRO(...)` invocations highlighted
- Expanded output showing what each macro generated
- Toggle to show/hide expansion per macro invocation
- GEN block output shown inline with assembler-colored syntax

## Pipeline Stage Mapping to UI

The 7-layer pipeline runs inside the COR24 emulator as a single compiler
execution. The Pipeline tab provides visibility into each stage:

| Stage | UI Display | Source |
|-------|-----------|--------|
| 1. Preprocess | Expanded .plsw with macro annotations | Compiler diagnostic output |
| 2. Lex | Token stream (keyword, ident, literal, etc.) | Compiler diagnostic output |
| 3. Parse | AST tree view (collapsible) | Compiler diagnostic output |
| 4. Semantic | Symbol table, type annotations | Compiler diagnostic output |
| 5. IR Lower | Intermediate representation | Compiler diagnostic output |
| 6. Reg Alloc | Register assignment map | Compiler diagnostic output |
| 7. Emit | Final .s assembly | Compiler UART output |

Note: Stages 2-6 require the PL/SW compiler to emit diagnostic output, which
may be added incrementally. Stage 1 (preprocess) and Stage 7 (emit) are
visible from the start.

## Yew Component Architecture

```
App (function_component)
  |
  +-- Header (title, tab bar)
  +-- TabContent
  |     +-- EditorTab (wizard + notebook cells)
  |     |     +-- WizardSidebar (step indicators, action button)
  |     |     +-- NotebookCells (scrolling container)
  |     |           +-- SourceCell (.plsw editor)
  |     |           +-- MacroCell (.msw editor, one per file)
  |     |           +-- PreprocessedCell (expanded output)
  |     |           +-- AssemblyCell (.s output)
  |     |           +-- DebugCell (listing + debugger)
  |     |                 +-- ControlBar (run/step/reset/speed)
  |     |                 +-- ListingPanel (assembled lines, PC highlight)
  |     |                 +-- RegisterPanel (r0-r7, PC, flags, heatmap)
  |     |                 +-- MemoryPanel (sparse hex dump)
  |     |                 +-- IoPanel (UART RX/TX, LED, switch)
  |     +-- PipelineTab (7-stage viewer)
  |           +-- StagePanel (one per pipeline stage)
  +-- Footer (license, links, build metadata)
```

# Product Requirements: web-sw-cor24-plsw

## Target User

Systems programming enthusiasts and retrocomputing hobbyists exploring the
COR24 24-bit RISC architecture. Users want to write PL/SW code in the browser,
see it compiled and run on the COR24 emulator, and understand the compilation
pipeline of this experimental PL/I-inspired language.

## Use Case

A browser-based PL/SW development environment that requires no local toolchain
installation. Users write PL/SW source and macro files, compile them through
the full 7-stage pipeline, view the generated COR24 assembly, and execute it
on the emulator -- all in one page.

## Core Features

### F1: PL/SW Source Editor
- Edit a single `.plsw` source file with syntax highlighting
- PL/SW keywords: DCL, PROC, DO, END, IF, THEN, ELSE, RETURNS, CALL, etc.
- Highlight macro invocations (`?MACRO(...)`) distinctly
- Highlight inline assembly blocks (`ASM DO; ... END;`)
- Highlight GEN blocks within macros

### F2: Macro File Editor
- Edit one or more `.msw` macro definition files
- Add/remove macro files from the project
- Macro syntax highlighting (MACRODEF, GEN, expansion directives)

### F3: Preprocessing Stage
- Run PL/SW preprocessor to expand macro invocations
- Display preprocessed PL/SW output as a notebook cell
- Collapsible macro expansion: show what each `?MACRO(...)` expanded to
- Scroll down to preprocessed output on completion

### F4: Compilation to Assembly
- Run the PL/SW compiler (on COR24 emulator) to produce `.s` assembly
- Display generated COR24 assembly as a notebook cell
- Scroll down to assembly output on completion
- Show compilation errors inline with source location

### F5: Assembly + Execution
- Assemble the generated `.s` using cor24-emulator's Rust assembler
- Display assembly listing with addresses and labels
- Run/Step/Reset controls for the emulator
- Register display (r0-r7, PC, condition flag) with change heatmap
- Memory viewer (sparse hex dump of SRAM and stack)
- UART I/O (TX output display, RX input field)
- Auto-scroll listing to current PC

### F6: Demo Programs
- Embedded demo PL/SW programs selectable from a dropdown
- Each demo includes .plsw source and any required .msw files
- Demos showcase language features: hello world, arithmetic, records,
  macros, inline assembly, etc.

### F7: File Upload
- Upload `.plsw` files from local filesystem
- Upload `.msw` macro files
- Replace current editor content with uploaded file

### F8: Pipeline Visualization (Tab 2)
- Second tab showing the 7-stage compiler pipeline
- Each stage displayed as a collapsible panel
- Stage 1 (Preprocess): macro expansion with annotations
- Stage 7 (Emit): final .s assembly
- Stages 2-6: diagnostic output as compiler support is added

### F9: Wizard-Style Progressive Workflow
- Left sidebar showing pipeline steps (Source, Macros, Preprocess,
  Compile, Assemble, Run)
- Action button advances to next step
- Completed steps clickable to scroll back
- Notebook cells revealed progressively as each step completes

## Non-Functional Requirements

### Performance
- Compilation via emulator should complete within a few seconds for
  typical programs (< 500 lines)
- Batch execution (50K+ instructions/tick) to prevent browser blocking
- WASM binary optimized for size (opt-level z, LTO)

### Browser Compatibility
- Modern browsers with WASM support (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge)
- No server backend required -- fully client-side

### Offline Capability
- Once loaded, the app works offline (all compilation happens in WASM)
- GitHub Pages deployment with static assets only

### Accessibility
- Monospace font for all code display
- High contrast Catppuccin Mocha dark theme
- Keyboard shortcuts for compile/run workflow

## Scope: v1

### In Scope
- Single .plsw file + multiple .msw files editing
- Full compile -> assemble -> run pipeline
- Demo programs with embedded source
- File upload for .plsw and .msw
- Assembly listing with debugger (run/step/reset)
- Register and memory display
- UART I/O
- Macro expansion view (collapsible)
- Pipeline tab (stages 1 and 7 initially; 2-6 as compiler adds diagnostics)

### Out of Scope (v1)
- Multi-file .plsw projects
- Persistent storage / save to disk
- Collaborative editing
- LSP-style code intelligence (autocomplete, go-to-definition)
- Source-level debugging (stepping through PL/SW, not COR24)
- Optimization passes or optimization visualization
- Mobile-optimized layout

## Success Criteria

1. User can write a PL/SW hello world program and see it compile and run
2. Demo programs all compile and execute correctly
3. Macro expansion is visible and collapsible
4. Assembly listing scrolls to current PC during stepping
5. Register heatmap shows recent changes
6. File upload works for .plsw and .msw files
7. Pipeline tab shows at minimum preprocessing and emission stages
8. Page loads and works offline after initial load
9. Deployed to GitHub Pages at the project URL

# Implementation Plan: web-sw-cor24-plsw

## Phase 1: Project Scaffold (Steps 1-2)

### Step 1: rust-project-init
Scaffold the Yew/WASM project with Trunk build system.

- Update Cargo.toml: Yew 0.21 (csr), wasm-bindgen, web-sys, gloo,
  console_error_panic_hook, cor24-emulator path dep, cor24-isa path dep
- Create lib crate type (cdylib + rlib)
- Create index.html with Catppuccin Mocha theme, Trunk directives
- Create src/main.rs (panic hook + Yew renderer)
- Create src/lib.rs with App function_component (header + footer + placeholder)
- Create scripts/serve.sh (port 9507) and scripts/build-pages.sh
- Create Trunk.toml with dist dir and public URL
- Create build.rs with git SHA, hostname, timestamp metadata
- Add .nojekyll, pages/ directory
- Verify `trunk build` succeeds, `./scripts/serve.sh` serves the page

### Step 2: wasm-build
Wire the cor24-emulator dependency and verify WASM integration.

- Import EmulatorCore and Assembler from cor24-emulator
- Create a minimal test: assemble a trivial COR24 program, load, step,
  read registers
- Verify the emulator runs in WASM (no panics, correct output)
- Add release profile (opt-level z, lto)

## Phase 2: Editor Panel (Steps 3-4)

### Step 3: yew-component -- Source Editor
Build the wizard sidebar + notebook cell layout with PL/SW source editor.

- Create 3-column grid layout (sidebar, wizard steps, notebook cells)
- Wizard sidebar with step indicators (Source, Macros, Preprocess,
  Compile, Assemble, Run)
- First notebook cell: textarea for .plsw source editing
- PL/SW syntax highlighting (keywords, macros, strings, comments, inline asm)
- Demo dropdown in sidebar or header to load example programs
- File upload button for .plsw files
- CSS styling: Catppuccin Mocha colors, monospace fonts

### Step 4: yew-component -- Macro File Editor
Add support for editing .msw macro files.

- Notebook cell(s) for .msw file editing below the .plsw cell
- Add/remove macro file buttons
- File upload for .msw files
- Macro syntax highlighting (MACRODEF, GEN, expansion directives)
- Each .msw file gets its own collapsible cell with filename header

## Phase 3: Compilation Pipeline (Steps 5-6)

### Step 5: wasm-build -- Compiler Integration
Wire the PL/SW compiler binary to run on the COR24 emulator.

- Embed pre-built plsw compiler binary in build.rs (or load from
  sibling project's build output)
- Create compilation workflow: load compiler binary -> feed .plsw source
  via UART RX -> capture .s assembly from UART TX
- Handle macro file feeding (concatenate .msw files before source, or
  use include mechanism)
- Error capture and display
- Notebook cell for preprocessed output (wizard step: Preprocess)
- Notebook cell for .s assembly output (wizard step: Compile)
- Scroll-to-cell on step completion

### Step 6: yew-component -- Macro Expansion View
Add collapsible macro expansion visualization.

- In the preprocessed output cell, annotate expanded macros
- Each `?MACRO(...)` invocation has a toggle to show/hide expansion
- GEN block output shown with assembly syntax coloring
- Collapsed by default, expandable per-invocation

## Phase 4: Assembly + Execution (Steps 7-8)

### Step 7: yew-component -- Assembly Listing + Debugger
Wire assembler and build the debugger panel.

- Assemble generated .s using cor24-emulator Assembler (Rust, not via
  emulator -- direct WASM call)
- Notebook cell for assembled listing with addresses
- Control bar: Run, Step (x1/x10/x100/x1K), Stop, Reset, speed slider
- Auto-scroll listing to current PC
- Current instruction highlighting
- Batch execution (50K instructions/tick) with gloo timer

### Step 8: yew-component -- Register + Memory + I/O
Add register display, memory viewer, and UART I/O.

- Register grid: r0-r7, PC, condition flag
- Change heatmap (hot = changed last step, warm = changed 2 steps ago)
- Sparse memory viewer (SRAM + stack regions)
- UART TX output display
- UART RX input field (type to send characters)
- Instruction count and emulator status (READY/RUNNING/HALTED)

## Phase 5: Pipeline Tab + Polish (Steps 9-10)

### Step 9: yew-component -- Pipeline Visualization Tab
Build the second tab showing 7-stage compiler pipeline.

- Tab bar in header (Editor | Pipeline)
- Pipeline tab with 7 collapsible stage panels
- Stage 1 (Preprocess): show macro expansion with annotations
- Stage 7 (Emit): show final .s assembly
- Stages 2-6: placeholder panels with descriptions of what each stage
  does, populated when compiler diagnostic output is available
- Each stage shows input -> output transformation

### Step 10: pre-commit -- Demo Programs + Polish
Finalize demos, build GitHub Pages, polish.

- Create embedded demo programs (hello, arithmetic, records, macros,
  inline asm)
- Each demo: name, description, .plsw source, optional .msw files
- Build to pages/ via build-pages.sh
- Verify GitHub Pages deployment
- Keyboard shortcuts (Ctrl+Enter to compile, F5 to run, F10 to step)
- Error display polish (inline error markers in editor)
- README with screenshot and link to live demo

## Phase 6+: Future Enhancements

- Source-level PL/SW debugging (step through PL/SW lines, not COR24)
- Pipeline stages 2-6 populated with real compiler diagnostics
- Mixed listing output (PL/SW source interleaved with generated assembly)
- Symbol table browser
- Code completion for PL/SW keywords and macro names
- Persistent editor state (localStorage)
- Multiple .plsw file support

# UI/UX Design: web-sw-cor24-plsw

## Layout Overview

The UI follows the cor24-rs wizard/notebook pattern: a 3-column grid with
progressive disclosure. The user advances through compilation stages via a
wizard sidebar, with each stage's output appearing as a notebook cell that
scrolls into view.

Two tabs provide different views of the same compilation:
- **Editor tab**: wizard-driven workflow from source to execution
- **Pipeline tab**: 7-stage compiler internals visualization

## Layout Wireframe

```
+-------+----------+--------------------------------------------------+
| Side  | Wizard   | Notebook Cells (scrollable)                      |
| bar   | Steps    |                                                  |
|       |          | +----------------------------------------------+ |
| Demos | [x] Src  | | .plsw Source Editor                          | |
| Upload|          | | [textarea with syntax highlighting]           | |
| Help  | [x] Macro| +----------------------------------------------+ |
|       |          | +----------------------------------------------+ |
|       | [ ] Pre  | | .msw Macro Files                             | |
|       |          | | [collapsible cells, one per .msw file]        | |
|       | [ ] Comp | +----------------------------------------------+ |
|       |          | +----------------------------------------------+ |
|       | [ ] Asm  | | Preprocessed PL/SW                           | |
|       |          | | [expanded source with macro annotations]      | |
|       | [ ] Run  | +----------------------------------------------+ |
|       |          | +----------------------------------------------+ |
|       | +------+ | | COR24 Assembly (.s)                          | |
|       | |Action| | | [generated assembly listing]                  | |
|       | |Button| | +----------------------------------------------+ |
|       | +------+ | +----------------------------------------------+ |
|       |          | | Debugger                                      | |
|       |          | | [controls] [listing | registers | memory]    | |
|       |          | +----------------------------------------------+ |
+-------+----------+--------------------------------------------------+
| Footer: MIT | (c) 2026 | COR24-TB | SHA | host | timestamp         |
+--------------------------------------------------------------------- +
```

## Catppuccin Mocha Theme Integration

All colors use CSS custom properties defined in index.html:

```css
:root {
    --crust:    #11111b;
    --mantle:   #181825;
    --base:     #1e1e2e;
    --surface0: #313244;
    --surface1: #45475a;
    --surface2: #585b70;
    --overlay0: #6c7086;
    --overlay1: #7f849c;
    --overlay2: #9399b2;
    --subtext0: #a6adc8;
    --subtext1: #bac2de;
    --text:     #cdd6f4;
    --lavender: #b4befe;
    --blue:     #89b4fa;
    --sapphire: #74c7ec;
    --sky:      #89dceb;
    --teal:     #94e2d5;
    --green:    #a6e3a1;
    --yellow:   #f9e2af;
    --peach:    #fab387;
    --maroon:   #eba0ac;
    --red:      #f38ba8;
    --mauve:    #cba6f7;
    --pink:     #f5c2e7;
    --flamingo: #f2cdcd;
    --rosewater:#f5e0dc;
    --mono: "JetBrains Mono", "Fira Code", "Cascadia Code", monospace;
}
```

### Color Assignments

| Element | Color | Variable |
|---------|-------|----------|
| Page background | #1e1e2e | --base |
| Sidebar background | #181825 | --mantle |
| Wizard step panel | #11111b | --crust |
| Cell header | --surface0 | #313244 |
| Cell background | --mantle | #181825 |
| Cell border | --surface1 | #45475a |
| Body text | --text | #cdd6f4 |
| Title/header | --blue | #89b4fa |
| Action button | --mauve | #cba6f7 |
| Completed step checkmark | --green | #a6e3a1 |
| Error text | --red | #f38ba8 |
| Warning text | --yellow | #f9e2af |
| Current PC line | --yellow bg at 15% opacity | |
| Register heatmap hot | --green | #a6e3a1 |
| Register heatmap warm | --yellow | #f9e2af |

## PL/SW Syntax Highlighting Scheme

### Keywords (--mauve / purple)
```
DCL DECLARE PROC PROCEDURE RETURNS DO END IF THEN ELSE WHILE
CALL GOTO RETURN STATIC AUTOMATIC EXTERNAL REGISTER BASED
BIT BYTE WORD INT CHAR PTR FIXED
```

### Macro Invocations (--peach / orange)
```
?GETMAIN  ?ESTAE  ?FREEMAIN  ?LINK  ?ENTRY  ?EXIT
```
Regex: `\?[A-Z][A-Z0-9_]*`

### Macro Definitions (--pink)
```
MACRODEF  MACROEND  GEN  ENDGEN
```

### Inline Assembly (--teal)
```
ASM DO; ... END;
```
The entire block is tinted with a subtle --teal background.

### GEN Blocks (--sky background tint)
Assembly code generated by macros, shown with a faint --sky background
to distinguish from handwritten assembly.

### String Literals (--green)
Single-quoted strings: `'Hello'`

### Numeric Literals (--peach)
Decimal, hex (0x...), binary (0b...)

### Comments (--overlay1 / dim)
```
/* block comments */
// line comments (if supported)
```

### Operators and Punctuation (--subtext1)
`;  (  )  ,  =  +  -  *  /  &  |  ^`

### Level Numbers (--yellow / bold)
```
DCL 1 RECORD,
      3 FIELD1 INT(24),
      3 FIELD2 CHAR(8);
```

## Macro Expansion Toggle Design

In the preprocessed output cell, each macro invocation is rendered as a
collapsible block:

```
  ?GETMAIN(LENGTH(256), SUBPOOL(0), ADDRESS(ptr));
  [v] Expansion (3 lines)                           <-- click to toggle
  |   LDA  r0, =256        ; LENGTH parameter
  |   LDA  r1, =0          ; SUBPOOL parameter
  |   JSR  r1, _svc_getmain
```

- Collapsed by default (shows invocation line only)
- Toggle arrow [>] / [v] on the expansion header
- Expansion lines indented with a left border in --peach
- GEN block output uses --sky left border
- Count of expanded lines shown in header

## Pipeline Stage Visualization (Tab 2)

The Pipeline tab shows all 7 compiler stages as vertical panels:

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
| Pipeline: hello.plsw                              |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|                                                    |
| [v] Stage 1: Macro Preprocessing                  |
| +------------------------------------------------+|
| | Input: .plsw + .msw files                      ||
| | Output: Expanded .plsw source                  ||
| | ?GETMAIN(...) -> [expanded 3 lines]            ||
| +------------------------------------------------+|
|                                                    |
| [v] Stage 2: Lexical Analysis                     |
| +------------------------------------------------+|
| | Token stream:                                   ||
| | DCL(kw) COUNT(ident) INT(kw) ((punc) 24(num)   ||
| +------------------------------------------------+|

## Layout Overview

The UI follows the cor24-rs wizard/notebook pattern: a 3-column grid with
progressive disclosure. The user advances through compilation stages via a
wizard sidebar, with each stage's output appearing as a notebook cell that
scrolls into view.

Two tabs provide different views of the same compilation:
- **Editor tab**: wizard-driven workflow from source to execution
- **Pipeline tab**: 7-stage compiler internals visualization

## PL/SW Syntax Highlighting Scheme

### Keywords (--mauve / purple)
```
DCL DECLARE PROC PROCEDURE RETURNS DO END IF THEN ELSE WHILE
CALL GOTO RETURN STATIC AUTOMATIC EXTERNAL REGISTER BASED
BIT BYTE WORD INT CHAR PTR FIXED
```

### Macro Invocations (--peach / orange)
```
?GETMAIN  ?ESTAE  ?FREEMAIN  ?LINK  ?ENTRY  ?EXIT
```
Regex: `\?[A-Z][A-Z0-9_]*`

### Macro Definitions (--pink)
```

```

### Inline Assembly (--teal)
```

```

# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)

Every agent session follows this 6-step loop. Do NOT skip or reorder steps.

1. **`agentrail next`** -- Read the current step prompt. The step prompt IS the instruction. Do not ask for confirmation; just do it.
2. **`agentrail begin`** -- Mark the step as in-progress. Do this immediately after reading.
3. **WORK** -- Implement what the step describes. Follow all project conventions below.
4. **`git commit`** -- Commit your work with a descriptive message. All pre-commit quality gates must pass.
5. **`agentrail complete`** -- Mark the step done. Include `--summary "..."`, `--reward N` (1-10), and `--actions N` (number of tool calls). If the step failed, use `--failure-mode "..."` and `--reward -1`.
6. **STOP** -- Do not continue. New work belongs in the next step (next session).

## Project: web-sw-cor24-plsw -- PL/SW Development Environment on COR24

Browser-based PL/SW development environment (editor/compiler) running the PL/SW compiler pipeline on the COR24 emulator via WASM. Built with Yew 0.21 CSR + Trunk.

PL/SW is a PL/I-inspired freestanding systems programming language for the COR24 24-bit RISC ISA. The web UI provides an editor with PL/SW syntax highlighting, compilation to COR24 assembly, assembly listing view, and execution on the COR24 emulator.

## Related Projects

All COR24 repos live under `~/github/sw-embed/` as siblings:

- `sw-cor24-plsw` -- PL/SW compiler (C, runs on COR24 natively)
- `sw-cor24-emulator` -- COR24 assembler and emulator (Rust)
- `sw-cor24-assembler` -- COR24 assembler library
- `web-sw-cor24-assembler` -- COR24 assembly IDE (browser)
- `web-sw-cor24-pcode` -- P-code VM debugger (browser, closest reference project)
- `sw-cor24-pcode` -- P-code VM, assembler, linker (Rust workspace)
- `sw-cor24-rust` -- Rust-to-COR24 pipeline

## Build

Edition 2024 for all Rust code. Never suppress warnings.

```bash
trunk build                    # Build WASM to dist/
./scripts/serve.sh             # Dev server
./scripts/build-pages.sh       # Release build to pages/ for GitHub Pages
cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings  # Lint
cargo fmt --all                # Format
```

## Available Task Types

rust-project-init, yew-component, wasm-build, rust-clippy-fix, pre-commit, rust-test-write, css-styling, documentation

## Architecture

- **Trunk** builds the WASM binary and serves it
- **cor24-emulator** provides `EmulatorCore` + `Assembler` (path dep to `../sw-cor24-emulator`)
- **Yew 0.21** CSR framework for the UI
- Component-based architecture using `Component` trait with `Msg` enum for state updates
- `build.rs` for compile-time pre-processing and metadata embedding
- Catppuccin Mocha dark theme
- `pages/` directory committed to git, deployed via GitHub Pages
- PL/SW compilation pipeline: source -> preprocess -> compile -> .s assembly -> assemble -> emulate -> output

## Key Conventions

- Monospace font stack: JetBrains Mono, Fira Code, Cascadia Code
- CSS custom properties for Catppuccin Mocha colors
- Flexbox layout for responsive multi-panel design
- `include_bytes!()` / `include!()` for embedding build-time outputs
- Release profile: `opt-level = "z"`, `lto = true` for minimal WASM size
- GitHub Pages URL: https://softwarewrighter.github.io/web-sw-cor24-plsw/

Browser-based PL/SW development environment (editor/compiler) running the PL/SW
compiler pipeline on the COR24 emulator via WASM. Built with Yew 0.21 CSR + Trunk.

PL/SW is a PL/I-inspired freestanding systems programming language for the COR24
24-bit RISC ISA. The web UI provides:
- Editor for one .plsw file and multiple .msw macro files

- Assembly listing + debugger with run/step, registers, memory, UART I/O

The PL/SW compiler is written in C and runs on the COR24 emulator in WASM
(same approach as the cor24-rs C pipeline).

## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)

Every agent session follows this 6-step loop. Do NOT skip or reorder steps.

1. **`agentrail next`** -- Read the current step prompt. The step prompt IS the instruction. Do not ask for confirmation; just do it.
